title: Rheoliadau Deddf Cydraddoldeb 2010 (Dyletswyddau Statudol) (Cymru) 2011 / Equality Act 2010 (Statutory Duties) (Wales) Regulations 2011
short: Dyletswyddau Cydraddoldeb Cymru / Welsh Specific Equality Duties
chapters: 20 rheoliad / regulations. These Regulations impose SPECIFIC DUTIES on Welsh public bodies to enable the better performance of the Public Sector Equality Duty (PSED) under Equality Act 2010 s.149. The Welsh specific duties are MORE PRESCRIPTIVE than the English equivalent — requiring Strategic Equality Plans, equality impact assessments, engagement provisions, employment information publication, pay difference analysis, and procurement equality considerations. CRITICAL FINDING: In 2018-19, the EHRC found that NONE of the 73 listed Welsh public bodies were FULLY COMPLIANT with the Welsh specific duties. This instrument is the Welsh equality framework that sits alongside the Well-being of Future Generations Act and the Social Partnership Act to form a comprehensive regulatory environment for Welsh public bodies and their supply chains.
bilingualNote: Mae'r Rheoliadau hyn ar gael yn Gymraeg ac yn Saesneg. / These Regulations are available in Welsh and English. Key terms are documented bilingually throughout.

parliamentaryOrigin.bill: Made by the Welsh Ministers under EqA 2010 ss.153-154
parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Welsh Ministers, using powers conferred by EqA 2010 s.153(2)
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: In force since 6 April 2011. The Regulations apply to 'listed authorities' — Welsh public bodies listed in Part 2 of Schedule 19 to the Equality Act 2010. The GENERAL duty (EqA s.149) applies UK-wide. The SPECIFIC duties (these Regulations) apply ONLY in Wales and are more prescriptive than the English specific duties (Equality Act 2010 (Specific Duties and Public Authorities) Regulations 2017).

significantAmendments.instrument: Socio-economic Duty (EqA Part 1, ss.1-3) — commenced in Wales from 31 March 2021
significantAmendments.effect: Wales was the FIRST part of the UK to commence the socio-economic duty (England commenced later in 2024). Public bodies in Wales must have due regard to the desirability of reducing inequalities of outcome resulting from socio-economic disadvantage when making strategic decisions. This COMPLEMENTS the specific equality duties.

precedingDocuments.title: Equality Act 2010, s.149 — Public Sector Equality Duty (General Duty)
precedingDocuments.significance: The UK-wide general duty. ALL public bodies must have due regard to: (a) eliminating discrimination, harassment, and victimisation; (b) advancing equality of opportunity; (c) fostering good relations. The Welsh specific duties provide the MECHANISM for Welsh bodies to demonstrate compliance with this general duty.

parts.title: Rhan 1 / Part 1 — Y Dyletswyddau Penodol: Beth Mae Cymru yn Ei Wneud yn Wahanol / The Specific Duties: What Wales Does Differently

sections.title: Y gwahaniaethau rhwng dyletswyddau Cymru a Lloegr / The differences between Welsh and English equality duties — and what employers must do
sections.summary: The Welsh specific duties go significantly FURTHER than the English equivalent. Wales requires Strategic Equality Plans, mandatory equality impact assessments (with publication), engagement provisions that mandate meaningful consultation, employment information publication including pay difference analysis across ALL protected characteristics (not just gender), and equality considerations in procurement. The English duties (2017 Regulations) require only: equality objectives every 4 years, annual information publication, and gender pay gap reporting for employers with 250+ staff.
sections.text:
CYMRU vs LLOEGR / WALES vs ENGLAND — KEY DIFFERENCES:

═══ 1. CYNLLUN CYDRADDOLDEB STRATEGOL / STRATEGIC EQUALITY PLAN (Reg.14) ═══

WALES: Every listed authority must publish a STRATEGIC EQUALITY PLAN (SEP / Cynllun Cydraddoldeb Strategol) — a single, comprehensive document setting out how the authority will meet the general duty. The SEP must include: equality objectives, the steps taken to achieve them, equality impact assessments, engagement provisions, employment information, pay difference analysis, and procurement equality considerations.

ENGLAND: No equivalent requirement. English bodies publish equality objectives but are not required to consolidate them into a strategic plan.

WHY IT MATTERS: The SEP provides a single point of accountability. Anyone — a citizen, a trade union, the EHRC — can read one document and assess whether the body is meeting its equality obligations. This transparency drives compliance.

═══ 2. AMCANION CYDRADDOLDEB / EQUALITY OBJECTIVES (Reg.3) ═══

WALES: Authorities must publish equality objectives designed to enable better performance of the general duty. If an authority does NOT publish an objective for a particular protected characteristic, it must EXPLAIN WHY. Objectives must be reviewed at least every 4 years (current cycle: April 2024, next April 2028). Each objective must include a statement of the steps to be taken, timescales, and monitoring arrangements.

ENGLAND: Similar requirement to publish objectives every 4 years, but no 'explain why not' requirement for missing characteristics.

═══ 3. ASESIADAU EFFAITH AR GYDRADDOLDEB / EQUALITY IMPACT ASSESSMENTS (Reg.8) ═══

WALES: Authorities MUST assess how new or proposed policies and practices may affect people with protected characteristics. Where the impact is SUBSTANTIAL, the authority must PUBLISH a report. This is a MANDATORY requirement — not discretionary.

ENGLAND: The English duties do not explicitly require EIAs. English bodies conduct them as good practice to demonstrate compliance with the general duty, but they are not a statutory specific duty.

WHY IT MATTERS: The Welsh mandatory EIA requirement means that EVERY significant policy decision — including workforce restructuring, service changes, and procurement decisions — must be assessed for equality impact BEFORE the decision is made. This is a powerful mechanism for preventing discriminatory outcomes.

═══ 4. DARPARIAETHAU YMGYSYLLTU / ENGAGEMENT PROVISIONS (Reg.5) ═══

WALES: When setting equality objectives and assessing impact, authorities must ENGAGE with people who share protected characteristics and those who represent them. This is a specific, mandatory engagement duty — not just a general consultation obligation.

ENGLAND: No equivalent specific engagement duty in the English regulations.

═══ 5. GWAHANIAETHAU CYFLOG / PAY DIFFERENCES (Regs.10-12) ═══

WALES: Authorities must have due regard to the need to address the causes of pay difference related to ANY protected characteristic — not just gender. Authorities must publish a GENDER PAY EQUALITY OBJECTIVE, or explain why they have not done so. The authority must publish an ACTION PLAN on pay difference including any policies relating to equal pay.

ENGLAND: Gender pay gap reporting for employers with 250+ staff (from 2017). No specific duty to address pay differences related to other protected characteristics.

WHY IT MATTERS: The Welsh duty is broader — it covers pay differences related to disability, ethnicity, age, and other protected characteristics, not just gender. This requires more comprehensive pay analysis.

═══ 6. CAFFAEL / PROCUREMENT (Reg.18) ═══

WALES: When procuring, authorities must have due regard to whether equality considerations should be included in AWARD CRITERIA (to help meet the general duty) and whether PERFORMANCE CONDITIONS should include equality considerations.

ENGLAND: No equivalent specific procurement duty in the English regulations (though the general duty applies to procurement decisions).

WHY IT MATTERS: Combined with the SPPP Act's socially responsible procurement duties, this creates a DOUBLE LAYER of procurement equality requirements in Wales. A contractor bidding for Welsh public contracts faces both fair work (SPPP Act) AND equality (EqA Welsh duties) evaluation criteria.

═══ 7. GWYBODAETH CYFLOGAETH / EMPLOYMENT INFORMATION (Regs.9-10) ═══

WALES: Authorities must collect and publish employment information showing how they meet the general duty as an employer. This includes: workforce composition by protected characteristic, recruitment data, training and development data, grievance and disciplinary data, and leavers data — all broken down by protected characteristic where possible.

ENGLAND: Annual information publication required but less prescriptive.

═══ 8. ADRODD BLYNYDDOL / ANNUAL REPORTING (Reg.16) ═══

WALES: Authorities must publish an annual report setting out progress against equality objectives, including: equality impact assessments conducted, engagement activities, employment information, pay difference data, and procurement equality actions.

ENGLAND: Annual information publication required.

═══ 9. Y DDYLETSWYDD ECONOMAIDD-GYMDEITHASOL / SOCIO-ECONOMIC DUTY (EqA Part 1) ═══

WALES: Commenced 31 March 2021 — FIRST in the UK. Public bodies must have due regard to the desirability of reducing inequalities of outcome resulting from socio-economic disadvantage when making STRATEGIC DECISIONS. This complements the specific equality duties.

ENGLAND: Commenced September 2024 — Wales was 3 years ahead.

WHAT THIS MEANS FOR EMPLOYERS:

For LISTED WELSH PUBLIC BODIES:
(a) You MUST have a Strategic Equality Plan — reviewed and republished every 4 years.
(b) You MUST conduct equality impact assessments on significant policy decisions.
(c) You MUST engage with people who share protected characteristics.
(d) You MUST publish employment information broken down by protected characteristic.
(e) You MUST analyse and publish pay difference data across all protected characteristics.
(f) You MUST publish a gender pay equality objective (or explain why not).
(g) You MUST include equality considerations in procurement.
(h) You MUST publish an annual report.

For PRIVATE SECTOR EMPLOYERS contracting with Welsh public bodies:
(a) Your bid may be evaluated against equality criteria (Reg.18).
(b) Your contract may include equality performance conditions.
(c) Combined with SPPP Act fair work clauses, your employment practices face DOUBLE SCRUTINY — fair work AND equality.
(d) Demonstrating strong equality practice strengthens your competitive position for Welsh public contracts.

EHRC COMPLIANCE FINDING (2018-19):

The Equality and Human Rights Commission conducted an extensive monitoring exercise in 2018-19 and found that NONE of the 73 listed Welsh public bodies were FULLY COMPLIANT with the Welsh specific duties. The EHRC recommended that the Welsh Government review how specific duties could be strengthened. This finding demonstrates that compliance is challenging — and that the EHRC is actively monitoring.

INTERACTION WITH THE WIDER WELSH FRAMEWORK:

The Welsh specific equality duties operate ALONGSIDE:
— The Well-being of Future Generations Act 2015 (well-being goals including 'A More Equal Wales')
— The Social Partnership Act 2023 (fair work, socially responsible procurement)
— The Welsh Language Standards (linguistic equality)
— The Socio-economic Duty (socio-economic disadvantage)

Together, these instruments create the most comprehensive public body equality and well-being framework in the UK. An HR leader in a Welsh public body must understand ALL of these obligations as an integrated system.
sections.keyPrinciple: The Welsh specific equality duties are MORE PRESCRIPTIVE than the English equivalent in every dimension: Strategic Equality Plans (not required in England), mandatory EIAs (not a specific duty in England), engagement provisions (unique to Wales), pay difference analysis across ALL protected characteristics (England only requires gender), procurement equality considerations (not a specific duty in England). Combined with the SPPP Act and WFG Act, this creates the most demanding equality framework for public bodies anywhere in the UK.

regulatoryGuidance.publisher: Comisiwn Cydraddoldeb a Hawliau Dynol / Equality and Human Rights Commission
regulatoryGuidance.title: Public Sector Equality Duty: specific duties in Wales — EHRC guidance
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The EHRC's authoritative guidance on the Welsh specific duties, including practical examples and compliance advice.

commonErrors: Assuming Welsh equality duties are the same as English duties — they are SIGNIFICANTLY more prescriptive. Wales requires Strategic Equality Plans, mandatory EIAs, engagement provisions, and broader pay difference analysis

Not knowing about the Strategic Equality Plan requirement — every listed Welsh authority must publish and maintain a SEP. This is the single most important compliance document for Welsh equality duties

Conducting equality impact assessments as optional or informal — in Wales, EIAs are a SPECIFIC STATUTORY DUTY. Where the impact is substantial, the EIA must be PUBLISHED. Not conducting or publishing EIAs is a regulatory failure

Only analysing gender pay differences — the Welsh duty covers pay differences related to ALL protected characteristics, not just gender. This requires more comprehensive pay analysis than the English equivalent

Not including equality considerations in procurement — Reg.18 specifically requires Welsh authorities to consider equality in award criteria and performance conditions. Combined with the SPPP Act, this creates a powerful procurement equality framework
Not knowing about the EHRC's 2018-19 finding — 0/73 Welsh public bodies were fully compliant. The EHRC is actively monitoring. Non-compliance is being tracked and reported

Not connecting the equality duties to the wider Welsh framework — the specific duties operate alongside the WFG Act (More Equal Wales goal), SPPP Act (fair work), Welsh Language Standards (linguistic equality), and socio-economic duty. They are not standalone — they are part of an integrated system
Assuming the socio-economic duty is new — Wales commenced it in March 2021, three years before England. Welsh public bodies have been working with this duty for 5 years

versions.text: IN FORCE: all regulations operational since 6 April 2011. Current equality objective cycle: April 2024-2028. Next SEP review due 2028. Socio-economic duty in force since 31 March 2021.
versions.amendedBy: No direct amendments — but complemented by the commencement of the Socio-economic Duty (EqA Part 1) in Wales from 31 March 2021
versions.policyRationale: The Welsh Government chose to impose more prescriptive specific duties than England, reflecting a stronger commitment to transparency and accountability in equality outcomes.

pendingAmendments.instrument: Welsh Government review of specific duties
pendingAmendments.status: Following the EHRC's 2018-19 finding of zero full compliance, the Welsh Government committed to reviewing how specific duties could be strengthened. Monitor for regulatory amendments.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — may result in stronger or additional duties

pendingAmendments.instrument: SPPP Act interaction — procurement equality + fair work
pendingAmendments.status: The SPPP Act's socially responsible procurement duties (commenced March 2026) create a DOUBLE LAYER with Reg.18 procurement equality. Monitor for integrated guidance.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — combined procurement obligations becoming more demanding

pendingAmendments.instrument: Welsh Government Strategic Equality and Human Rights Plan 2025-2029
pendingAmendments.status: Published with 7 National Equality Objectives. Monitor for how this shapes individual public body SEPs.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — sets national direction for equality priorities

surveillanceStatus.status: IN FORCE — mature framework (since 2011), EHRC actively monitoring compliance
